name: Tiered sensing deployment and escalation protocol
consequences: Shifting to a two-tier regime increases data density for routine nights while limiting intrusiveness on most nights. Downstream, fuller PSG on escalation nights improves diagnostic resolution for flagged events but demands more technician time and higher equipment utilization, potentially reducing overall nightly capacity.
options: Implement a two-tier monitoring regime that prioritizes dry-electrode EEG headbands and mattress sensors for the majority of nights, reserving full PSG on escalation nights when flagged events occur.
Adopt a fixed nightly schedule for enhanced PSG to occur every third night across all participants, ensuring sufficient data density while maintaining participant comfort and safety margins.
Introduce randomization of escalation nights across participants to prevent systematic bias and assess the impact of fuller PSG on event capture without inducing Hawthorne effects.
review: Core tension: data yield vs participant burden and scheduling feasibility. Weakness: none of the options explicitly account for day-to-day variability in sleep schedules or hospital operational disruptions that can affect escalation fidelity.

name: Recruitment cadence and control-cohort integration
consequences: Prioritizing recruitment channels aligned to the Bonn ecosystem accelerates enrollment and improves screening consistency. Downstream, faster enrollment may compress time for thorough data quality checks and increase risk of selection bias if eligibility criteria are tightened too aggressively.
options: Coordinate recruitment through the Bonn sleep clinic and regional networks to concentrate on high-probability candidates with clear pre-admission pathways, accelerating enrollment velocity.
Implement a rolling 18-week recruitment window with interim reviews to adjust eligibility criteria and broaden the pool of eligible participants if early screening yields lag.
Incorporate a matched control recruitment stream in parallel and align consent workflows to optimize data quality and comparability across cohorts.
review: Core tension: enrollment velocity vs representativeness. Weakness: options lack explicit safeguards against selection bias or demographic imbalances.

name: Data governance and privacy framework
consequences: Strengthening governance clarifies data access and sharing boundaries, enabling external validation while preserving privacy. Downstream, stricter controls may slow collaborations and extend timelines for data-use agreements, potentially delaying publications.
options: Institute a staged data access protocol that grants researchers tiered read permissions, ensuring de-identified sensor data is shareable while preserving video privacy.
Deploy automated redaction and consent-tracking systems to maintain compliance with privacy restrictions, enabling smoother external collaborations within approved data-use agreements.
Pursue a data-sharing framework that deposits de-identified physiological data into recognized repositories, while keeping video and identifying metadata locally under governance review.
review: Core tension: enabling external validation while preserving privacy. Weakness: governance timelines and ethics approvals are omitted.

name: Staffing model and shift design
consequences: Reconfiguring staffing improves 24/7 observation coverage and safety margins while balancing operational risk. Downstream, higher staffing flexibility can increase personnel costs and require more robust fatigue management and turnover controls.
options: Reorganize staffing into overlapping eight-hour shifts with on-call coverage to ensure 24/7 observation capability while maintaining clinician safety margins.
Cross-train technicians to perform both scoring and device maintenance, reducing handoffs and improving data throughput during high-activity weeks.
Introduce a floating backfill role to maintain continuity during turnover while enforcing strict onboarding and ongoing ethics training to safeguard data integrity.
review: Core tension: safe staffing vs cost. Weakness: no explicit minimum clinician-to-participant ratios or fatigue thresholds.

name: Adaptive sample sizing and interim evaluation
consequences: Instituting interim analyses helps avoid misalignment between enrollment pace and data yield, enabling resource reallocation as needed. Downstream, adaptive decisions may introduce governance complexity and potential delays if pre-specified criteria are not clear.
options: Establish predefined interim analyses at 6 and 12 months to recalibrate target enrollment and event-capture thresholds using observed event yields, guiding resource allocation.
Implement a protocol-triggered recruitment-review process at 18 months if events per participant fall below a threshold, enabling scope adjustment or criteria refinement.
Develop a decision framework that uses interim data to realign aims toward phenotyping depth rather than recruitment quantity, preserving study power.
review: Core tension: balancing depth of phenotyping with event yield. Weakness: lacks explicit stopping rules or pre-registered adaptive criteria.

name: Semi-automated event triage development and validation
consequences: Developing a semi-automated triage aims to rank probable parasomnia episodes, reducing manual review workload while maintaining scoring accuracy. Downstream, performance depends on robust validation and consistent inter-rater reliability, with risks if models overfit to initial data.
options: Prototype and validate a semi-automated event-triage workspace that ranks likely parasomnia episodes, reducing manual review time while maintaining dual-scoring accuracy.
Pilot a cross-lab benchmarking study with an external partner to assess generalizability of the triage model before full deployment.
Invest in an auditable annotation workflow with synchronized timing metadata and structured scoring templates to improve inter-rater reliability and data traceability.
review: Core tension: reliability of semi-automated scoring vs manual gold standard. Weakness: insufficient plan for handling conflicting automated scores or edge cases.

name: Residential safety and privacy guardrails expansion
consequences: Enhancing residential safety and privacy guardrails reduces the risk of incidents and unauthorized data exposure during long stays. It also increases operational overhead for audits, training, and access controls, potentially slowing data access but improving participant trust and regulatory compliance.
options: Implement tiered privacy-preserving data access with role-based permissions, local de-identification, and audit trails to minimize participant risk while preserving research value.
Add automated safety lapse detection and escalation triggers tied to nurse-driven incident response protocols to reduce risk during high-sensitivity nights.
Institute independent data access reviews and privacy impact assessments on a quarterly basis to align with evolving regulatory expectations and maintain public trust.
review: Core tension: strengthening safety and privacy can slow data access and analysis. Weakness: the options do not quantify the incremental staffing or training burden, or the impact on recruitment velocity.

name: Scaled recruitment funnel and onboarding milestones
consequences: Scaled recruitment funnel and onboarding milestones create higher-quality cohorts and reduce early dropout, improving downstream data integrity and study power. The approach introduces additional administrative steps and longer onboarding timelines, which may constrain speed to first data capture.
options: Institute staged consent and onboarding milestones tied to pre-screen data quality checks to improve enrollment yield and early data integrity.
Pilot regional outreach partnerships with local clinicians to diversify referrals and reduce time-to-enrollment bottlenecks.
Deploy flexible compensation adjustments balanced with budget discipline and regulatory compliance to sustain participation without inflating costs.
review: Core tension: reliance on external partnerships may introduce coordination delays; Weakness: the options overlook explicit metrics for referral quality and regulatory alignment.

name: Adaptive annotation throughput and quality assurance regime
consequences: Adaptive annotation throughput and quality assurance regime improves data labeling speed and reliability, enabling more timely analyses. It also requires sustained training and monitoring, which may divert time from recruitment or facility maintenance.
options: Institute continuous training cycles for annotators with blinded dual scoring and consensus reviews to improve inter-rater reliability.
Integrate a rotating annotation audit desk with weekly feedback loops to catch drift and maintain scoring consistency.
Pilot cross-site annotation tasks with external experts to diversify perspectives and accelerate labeling for rarer event types.
review: Core tension: human-centered scoring is a bottleneck requiring significant time; Weakness: none of the options address automation or active learning to reduce manual workload.

name: Modular sensing architecture and data interoperability standardization
consequences: Modular sensing architecture and data interoperability standardization reduces vendor lock-in and accelerates upgrades, enabling protocol iterations with less integration risk. It also requires upfront design work and validation to ensure interoperability across devices and data integrity.
options: Standardize sensor interfaces and data schemas to enable plug-and-play upgrades and interchangeability across equipment vendors.
Adopt an EEG-BIDS compatible data pipeline with automated QC checks to minimize manual processing time.
Establish a vendor-agnostic telemetry layer that supports rapid decommissioning and reconfiguration without protocol disruption.
review: Core tension: standardization can constrain device-specific performance and delay procurement; Weakness: options do not lay out a plan for ongoing calibration costs or vendor risk management.

name: Pilot-driven capacity planning and staged expansion trigger
consequences: Pilot-driven capacity planning and staged expansion trigger aligns infrastructure growth with demonstrated outcomes, reducing overbuild risk and budget waste. It also introduces potential delays if thresholds are not met and requires robust forecasting and monitoring.
options: Define explicit KPIs and a go/no-go calendar to expand from 8 to 12 suites only after data quality benchmarks are met.
Create a probabilistic capacity model incorporating variable stay durations to forecast occupancy and staffing needs.
Pre-negotiate lease extensions and conversion options with landlords to preserve flexibility if program success warrants acceleration.
review: Core tension: expansion hinges on performance milestones, which may conflict with urgent research needs; Weakness: does not address budget reallocation governance or contingency plans if funding decreases.

name: Strategic governance and stakeholder alignment framework
consequences: Strategic governance and stakeholder alignment framework clarifies decision rights and milestone expectations, reducing drift and funding risk. It also adds governance overhead and can slow urgent reallocations in response to safety concerns or data findings.
options: Formalize quarterly progress reviews with external advisory board milestones to align funding, ethics, and publication objectives.
Draft formal escalation paths for budget variances and staffing shortages to maintain project continuity.
Publish a transparent data sharing and authorship policy to manage expectations with industry partners and sponsors.
review: Core tension: governance layers risk slowing critical decisions when urgent actions are needed; Weakness: options do not specify mechanisms for exception handling or rapid reallocation during emergencies.

name: Operational risk hedging through flexible staffing and cross-training
consequences: Pulling this lever increases resilience to staffing gaps and supports scalable expansion as data quality permits. It may entail higher training costs and more complex scheduling, which could delay full implementation if staffing pools are slow to onboard.
options: Establish cross-trained night shift teams with rotating roles to sustain safe monitoring coverage across eight suites while maintaining scalable capacity for phased expansion.
Contract a preferred staffing pool and implement a tiered overtime policy to balance coverage during peaks without compromising patient safety or staff well-being.
Introduce formal handoff protocols and fatigue-aware shift scheduling to reduce miscommunication during high-load nights while awaiting longer-term automation.
review: Core tension centers on scaling safety-critical coverage while keeping labor costs and onboarding time in check; a weakness is that none of the options explicitly price risk of extended onboarding or vendor reliability.

name: Expanded data capture scope versus privacy risk
consequences: Expanding data capture improves event characterization and model development but increases privacy risk and data governance burden. It may require additional storage and processing capacity, and could necessitate revised consent and ethics approvals.
options: Pilot enhanced nights with full PSG on a randomized subset to validate event triage while preserving privacy constraints.
Scale sensor suite to include environmental and ambient data across all rooms to improve phenotyping.
Limit data scope to de-identified datasets with strict access controls and data-use agreements to minimize privacy exposure.
review: Core tension is between data richness and privacy governance; weakness: none of the options detail how consent mechanisms or participant opt-out rights will be updated for expanded data capture.

name: Clinical governance integration with external scientific advisory board
consequences: Bringing external oversight can improve transparency and scientific rigor but may slow decision cycles if advisor availability is inconsistent. It also requires clear scope and budgeting to avoid drift from core aims.
options: Formalize quarterly governance reviews that explicitly tie project milestones to ethical compliance updates and funding progress.
Engage external clinicians for blinded second opinions on adjudication to improve reliability at the cost of timeline.
Create a rotating governance liaison role embedded in PI's team to accelerate decision-making with stakeholder input.
review: External oversight enhances quality but can impede momentum; weakness: options do not specify contingency paths if external reviews miss milestones.

name: Participant selection criteria refinement to optimize event yield
consequences: Tightening inclusion criteria can raise average event yield per enrollee but may reduce eligible population and slow enrollment. Conversely, relaxing criteria can broaden recruitment but may introduce heterogeneity that complicates adjudication.
options: Adopt stricter inclusion criteria to target higher event frequency while ensuring representativeness remains acceptable overall.
Relax exclusion criteria for minor comorbidities to increase eligible pool without compromising safety significantly overall.
Implement adaptive recruitment quotas aligned with interim event yield and safety review to optimize throughput.
review: Core tension involves representativeness versus event yield; weakness: options don't address potential recruitment channel biases that could affect sample diversity.

name: Residential environment design balancing naturalistic behavior and safety constraints
consequences: Design decisions affect how naturally participants behave and how complete the data are; balancing ambience with safety can improve data fidelity but may increase construction and maintenance costs. Privacy considerations also shape what can be observed and reviewed.
options: Invest in controlled acoustic and lighting tweaks to simulate home-like ambience without increasing risk during episodes.
Implement modular room dividers and visual cues to reduce wandering while maintaining domestic feel and comfort.
Introduce anonymized video review protocols with privacy-centric access to ensure safety without public exposure online.
review: Core tension: environment realism versus safety constraints; weakness: options do not fully address staff training needs for enforcing new room configurations during events.

name: Semi-automated event triage tool development governance
consequences: This lever can reduce manual review burden and speed up triage but risks over-reliance on imperfect automation if thresholds are not carefully tuned. It also demands robust privacy and data governance for external benchmarking.
options: Pilot a dual-human scoring benchmark with a separate blinded adjudication layer to validate triage outputs.
Open a small, strictly de-identified data subset for external algorithm benchmarking under data-use agreements safeguarding privacy.
Institute a staged rollout with continuous feedback loops from technicians to iterate scoring rules safely.
review: Core tension: automation balance with human oversight; weakness: options miss contingency plans if adjudication backlog occurs or if performance plateaus.
